instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
IRDAI's New Rules on Commissions Give Companies Flexibility
The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) is the insurance regulator in India which ensures and promotes the orderly growth of the insurance industry. IRADI sets rules and regulations for insurance companies for their operation. Recently, IRDAI has set new rules on commissions charged by insurance companies. Let’s take a look at the new rules on commission and what impact they can have on the insurance companies and the policyholder.
In a recent draft notification issued by the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), it has proposed to cap the commissions, rewards, and remuneration to the insurance agents. High commission rates, specifically in the initial years have been the tender spots for the policyholders over the long term. This in many cases can lead to discontinuation of life insurance policies or non-renewal of the insurance policy. Hence, IRDAI has proposed a new rule on commissions that can boost the persistency ratio.
What is the persistency ratio?
Persistency ratio refers to the ratio of insurance policies receiving premium payments on time in a year. It is the ratio that shows the total number of policies that an insurance company has to the number of net active policies (renewed/in-force).
IRDAI’s rules on commission caps not only would encourage a higher persistency ratio, but also would give flexibility to insurance companies in rationalizing the payments to their agents. Let’s first take a look at the proposed commission rules by the IRDAI.
IRDAI’s new rules on commission1
For life insurance policies
Commission cap on additional commission or remuneration paid for regular premium/limited premium payment policies
For general insurance and health insurance policies
The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) has set a new rule for general insurance companies do not charge commissions or pay rewards or remuneration of more than 20% of the gross written premium on general insurance and health insurance policies. The new rule has reduced the commission rate to 20% from the 30% to 35% which they would charge earlier.
If we look at the proposed grid, the first-year commission on regular premium life insurance policies has reduced from the peak of 40% to 20%. The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) has allowed life insurance companies whose expenses of management (EoM) are under 70% of the allowable limit to set their own commission rates across the life insurance product category. Expenses of Management (EoM) of life insurance companies include expenses like a commission paid to intermediaries, brokerage or remuneration, administration cost, and rewards to agents. Life insurance companies whose expenses of management (EoM) exceed 70% of the permitted limit have to follow the maximum commission rate outlined by the insurance regulator, IRDAI.
Impact of IRDAI’s New Rules on Capping the Commission
The following are the possible impact of IRDAI’s new rules on setting the ceiling on the commission to intermediaries.
The stringent ceiling set on the commission payable to intermediaries by the life insurance and general insurance companies can benefit the policyholders in terms of timely renewal and keep the policy in force without worrying about the heavy upfront cost. However, a change in the commission grid may not impact them in a big way but surely can help over the long run.
A new commission grid can definitely give flexibility to insurance companies whose expenses of management (EoM) are under 70% of the allowable limit to structure their own commission grid across the life insurance product category. However, life insurance companies whose expenses of management (EoM) are exceeding 70% of the allowable limit, only have to face the restrictions.
A new commission grid proposed by the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), might disincentivize the small agents from selling life insurance policies.
To sum up, this new commission rule of IRDAI would give full flexibility to the big insurance players to set their own commission structure. However, it would discourage small agents and brokers and they would avoid selling insurance policies. This long overdue reform which is proposed by the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), there would effectively rationalize the system of commission, remuneration, and rewards to agents in the insurance industry.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
